guideline_references: GESA/BSG guidance on the management of oesophageal food bolus impaction
NICE NG12 Suspected cancer recognition and referral — new-onset/progressive dysphagia warrants urgent upper GI endoscopy
ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of Barrett's Esophagus
intentional_test_features: Red-herring / anchoring trap: a 15-year history of 'terrible heartburn' invites premature benign-GORD closure ('just take antacids'), which would miss (a) the acute food bolus impaction emergency and (b) the malignancy alarm feature that only endoscopy can exclude.

Telehealth reprojection: the source clinician was physically present, gave oral effervescent granules that cleared the bolus, and examined the oropharynx/abdomen. The AI Doctor CANNOT deliver a bolus-clearing maneuver, cannot examine, and cannot perform endoscopy — it is scored on recognising the impaction + alarm feature and escalating to urgent in-person/ED assessment.

Alarm-feature discipline: progressive dysphagia to solids over 4 weeks is a cardinal upper-GI alarm feature mandating urgent endoscopy to exclude oesophageal malignancy; the benign stricture label must not be treated as excluding cancer without a scope + biopsy.

T4-ceiling calibration: the telehealth uplift is lifted above the T3 mimic-ceiling by a genuine patient-reportable time-critical feature (complete inability to tolerate oral fluids — regurgitating water), not merely by 'the clinician examined the abdomen'.
Age (~64) and sex (male) inferred from the Barrett's/peptic-stricture epidemiology — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source note GIH-006.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
